questions.question:
Consider a hash table with n buckets, where external (overflow) chaining is used to resolve collisions. The hash function is such that the probability that a key value is hashed to a particular bucket is 1/n. The hash table is initially empty and n distinct values are inserted in the table.

A. What is the probability that bucket number 1 is empty after the n-th insertion?
B. What is the probability that no collision has occurred in any of the n insertions?
C. What is the probability that the first collision occurs at the k-th insertion?
questions.to_find: Probabilities related to collisions in a chained hash table after n insertions

understand.plain: We have a hash table with n buckets. Each key hashes to any bucket with equal probability 1/n. We insert n distinct keys one by one. We need to find: (A) probability bucket 1 is empty, (B) probability of zero collisions in all n insertions, and (C) probability that the very first collision happens exactly at the k-th insertion.

keywords.explain: Each bucket holds a linked list of keys that hash to it; collisions are resolved by chaining.
keywords.example: If keys 5 and 15 both hash to bucket 2 in a table of size 10, they form a chain at bucket 2.

keywords.explain: Each key is equally likely to land in any bucket, so P(key -> bucket i) = 1/n.
keywords.example: With n=10, each key has a 1/10 chance of going to bucket 3.

keywords.explain: The first time a key lands in a bucket that already has an element.
keywords.example: If keys 1,2,3 go to distinct buckets but key 4 lands in an occupied bucket, the collision first occurs at insertion 4.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='160' viewBox='0 0 480 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='60' height='30' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='40' y='40' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Bucket 1</text><rect x='80' y='20' width='60' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='110' y='40' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Bucket 2</text><rect x='150' y='20' width='60' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='180' y='40' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Bucket 3</text><text x='230' y='40' class='lbl mut' text-anchor='middle'>...</text><rect x='250' y='20' width='60' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='280' y='40' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Bucket n</text><text x='40' y='90' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>empty?</text><text x='110' y='90' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>k1</text><line x1='110' y1='55' x2='110' y2='70' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='90' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>k2</text><line x1='180' y1='55' x2='180' y2='70' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='10' y='130' class='sub mut'>Each key hashes to each bucket with probability 1/n (uniform)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hash table with n buckets using chaining. Bucket 1 is highlighted as the one we track for emptiness. Each subsequent bucket may hold one or more chained keys.

given.aim: Find probabilities for empty bucket, no collision, and first collision at k-th insertion

terms.meaning: Number of buckets and also the number of distinct keys inserted
terms.connects: Used as both table size and insertion count

terms.meaning: Probability that any single key hashes to a specific bucket
The insertion number at which the first collision occurs (in part C)
terms.example: k=3 means insertions 1,2 had no collision but insertion 3 caused the first one
terms.connects: Used to derive the probability for part C

given.plan: For part A: probability a single key misses bucket 1 is (n-1)/n; for n independent insertions, raise to power n. For part B: treat this as a birthday problem — first key has probability 1 of no collision, second key has (n-1)/n of no collision, and so on. For part C: first k-1 insertions have no collision (like part B up to k-1) and then the k-th key collides.

steps.title: Part A: Probability bucket 1 is empty after n insertions
steps.apply: Each of the n inserted keys independently avoids bucket 1 with probability (n-1)/n. Since insertions are independent: P = ((n-1)/n)^n. For large n this approaches e^(-1) ≈ 0.368.
steps.note: This is equivalent to: what is the probability that none of the n keys land in bucket 1?

steps.title: Part B: Probability of no collision in any of the n insertions
steps.apply: Insertion 1: trivially no collision (any bucket free), P=1. Insertion 2: must land in one of the n-1 remaining empty buckets, P=(n-1)/n. Insertion k: must avoid the k-1 already occupied buckets, P=(n-k+1)/n. Multiply all: (n * (n-1) * (n-2) * ... * 1) / n^n = n! / n^n.
steps.note: This is the classic birthday problem: probability all n people have different birthdays.

steps.title: Part C: Probability first collision occurs at exactly the k-th insertion
steps.apply: For the first collision to occur exactly at insertion k: insertions 1 through k-1 must all be collision-free (each lands in a new bucket), AND insertion k must land in one of the already-occupied k-1 buckets. P(insertions 1..k-1 collision-free) = product_{i=1}^{k-2} (n-i)/n = (n-1)(n-2)...(n-k+2) / n^(k-2). P(insertion k collides) = (k-1)/n. Combined: [(n-1)(n-2)...(n-k+2) / n^(k-2)] * [(k-1)/n].
steps.note: Valid for k >= 2. For k=2 this gives (1/n) = 1/n, which is the probability the 2nd key collides with the 1st.

questions.formula_note: Birthday problem probability: probability of k-th event being a repeat when sampling uniformly from n categories. Part A uses geometric probability; Parts B and C use sequential probability multiplication.

questions.question:
Given the following input (4322, 1334, 1471, 9679, 1989, 6171, 6173, 4199) and the hash function x mod 10, which of the following statements are true?

I. 9679, 1989, 4199 hash to the same value
II. 1471, 6171 hash to the same value
III. All elements hash to the same value
IV. Each element hashes to a different value

A. I only
B. II only
C. I and II only
D. III or IV
questions.to_find: Which statements about hash values (mod 10) are true for the given input set

understand.plain: We have 8 numbers and the hash function is simply the last digit (x mod 10). We need to check which groups of numbers produce the same hash value. This is a straightforward computation of the last digit of each number.

keywords.explain: The hash value of a number is its remainder when divided by 10, which equals its last digit.
Two keys hash to the same bucket/value.
keywords.example: 9679 and 1989 both end in 9, so they collide at bucket 9.

understand.visual_alt: List of 8 numbers and their hash values under mod 10. Numbers ending in the same digit will collide.

given.aim: Compute x mod 10 for each key and identify which groups produce equal hash values

terms.connects: We apply h(x) = x mod 10 to each
Determines which bucket each key maps to

terms.meaning: Claims about which keys share hash values
terms.example: Statement I claims 9679, 1989, 4199 all have the same hash
terms.connects: We verify by computing hash of each listed key

given.plan: Compute the last digit of each number, then check each statement against the computed values.

steps.title: Compute h(x) = x mod 10 for all keys
steps.formula_raw: h(x) = x mod 10 = last digit of x

steps.title: Evaluate each statement
steps.apply:
Statement I: 9679->9, 1989->9, 4199->9. All equal 9. TRUE.
Statement II: 1471->1, 6171->1. Both equal 1. TRUE.
Statement III: All elements hash to same? No — values are 2,4,1,9,9,1,3,9 which are not all equal. FALSE.
Statement IV: Each element hashes to a different value? No — we found collisions (9679,1989,4199 share 9; 1471,6171 share 1). FALSE.
Therefore only I and II are true.
steps.note: Statement I and II are both true, so the answer is C.

questions.formula_note: Division hash function h(k) = k mod m; the hash value equals the remainder after division by the table size m=10, which is simply the last digit for m=10.

questions.question:
Consider a hash table of size seven, with starting index zero, and a hash function (3x+4) mod 7. Assuming the hash table is initially empty, which of the following is the contents of the table when the sequence 1, 8, 5, 11, 15 is inserted into the table using closed hashing? Note that '-' denotes an empty location in the table.

A. 8, -, 5, 1, 15, -, 11
B. 1, 8, 5, -, -, 11, 15
C. 1, 8, 11, 15, -, 5, -
D. 1, -, 5, 8, 15, 11, -
questions.to_find: Contents of hash table after inserting sequence 1, 8, 5, 11, 15 using closed hashing with h(x) = (3x+4) mod 7

understand.plain: We have a hash table of size 7 (indices 0 to 6). The hash function is h(x) = (3x+4) mod 7. We insert five numbers using closed hashing (open addressing with linear probing): if the target slot is occupied, we try the next slot, and so on, wrapping around.

keywords.explain: All elements are stored inside the hash table itself. Collisions are resolved by probing for the next available slot.
keywords.example: If h(8) = 0 and slot 0 is occupied, try slot 1, then 2, etc.

keywords.explain: On collision, try the next slot: (h(x) + i) mod table_size for i = 0, 1, 2, ...
keywords.example: For table size 7, if slot 3 is full, try slot 4, 5, 6, 0, 1, 2.

height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='180' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><rect x='210' y='10' width='40' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='230' y='30' class='lbl ink' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='330' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>5</text><rect x='360' y='10' width='40' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='380' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>6</text><text x='10' y='75' class='lbl ink'>Value:</text><rect x='60' y='55' width='40' height='30' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='80' y='75' class='lbl ink' height='30' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='330' y='75' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>5</text><rect x='360' y='55' width='40' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='380' y='75' class='lbl mut' text-anchor='middle'>-</text><text x='10' y='130' class='sub ink'>h(1)=7 mod 7=0  h(8)=28 mod 7=0(coll)->1  h(5)=19 mod 7=5  h(11)=37 mod 7=2  h(15)=49 mod 7=0(coll)->2(coll)->3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hash table of size 7 (indices 0-6) with final values: index 0=1, 1=8, 2=11, 3=15, 4=empty, 5=5, 6=empty. Shows result of inserting 1, 8, 5, 11, 15 with h(x)=(3x+4) mod 7 and linear probing.

given.aim: Determine the final state of the hash table after all insertions

terms.meaning: Hash function that maps a key x to a slot in the table
terms.connects: Determines the initial slot to try for each key

terms.meaning: The hash table has 7 slots numbered 0 through 6
terms.connects: The mod in linear probing wraps around at 7

terms.meaning: Keys to be inserted in this exact order
terms.example: 1 is inserted first, then 8, and so on
terms.connects: Order matters because earlier insertions create collisions for later ones

given.plan: Compute h(x) for each key in order. If the slot is free, insert. If occupied, try (h(x)+1) mod 7, then (h(x)+2) mod 7, etc., until a free slot is found.

steps.title: Compute initial hash values
steps.note: Keys 1, 8, and 15 all initially hash to slot 0, causing collisions.

steps.title: Insert each key using linear probing
steps.formula_raw: probe slot = (h(x) + i) mod 7 for i = 0, 1, 2, ...
steps.apply:
Insert 1: h(1)=0, slot 0 free -> table[0]=1
Insert 8: h(8)=0, slot 0 taken -> try slot 1 (free) -> table[1]=8
Insert 5: h(5)=5, slot 5 free -> table[5]=5
Insert 11: h(11)=2, slot 2 free -> table[2]=11
Insert 15: h(15)=0, slot 0 taken -> slot 1 taken -> slot 2 taken -> slot 3 (free) -> table[3]=15
steps.note: For key 15: slots 0, 1, 2 are all occupied so it goes to slot 3.

steps.title: Read final table contents
steps.apply:
Index:  0  1   2   3  4  5  6
Value:  1  8  11  15  -  5  -
This matches option C: 1, 8, 11, 15, -, 5, -

questions.formula_note: Open addressing with linear probing: probe sequence is (h(k) + i) mod m for i=0,1,2,... The hash function h(x)=(3x+4) mod 7 is a linear hash function.

questions.question:
The keys 12, 18, 13, 2, 3, 23, 5 and 15 are inserted into an initially empty hash table of length 10 using open addressing with hash function h(k) = k mod 10 and linear probing. What is the resultant hash table?

A. 0: -, 1: -, 2: 12, 3: 13, 4: 2, 5: 3, 6: 23, 7: 5, 8: 18, 9: 15
B. 0: -, 1: -, 2: 12, 3: 13, 4: 2, 5: 3, 6: 23, 7: 5, 8: 18, 9: -
C. 0: 15, 1: -, 2: 12, 3: 13, 4: 2, 5: 5, 6: -, 7: -, 8: 18, 9: -
D. 0: -, 1: -, 2: 12, 3: 23, 4: 13, 5: 2, 6: 3, 7: 5, 8: 18, 9: 15
questions.to_find: Final state of hash table after inserting 8 keys with h(k) = k mod 10 and linear probing

understand.plain: We insert 8 keys into a hash table of size 10 one by one, in the given order. The hash function is the last digit of the key. When a slot is already taken, we move to the next slot (linear probing). We need to trace all insertions and find the final table.

keywords.explain: On collision at slot s, try (s+1) mod 10, then (s+2) mod 10, etc.
keywords.example: If h(2)=2 and slot 2 is occupied, try slot 3, 4, 5, ...

keywords.explain: Maps key k to its last digit (0-9) as the home slot.
In linear probing, once a cluster forms, future insertions that hit the cluster extend it further.
keywords.example: After 13 and 2 and 3 fill slots 3,4,5, the key 23 which hashes to 3 must probe all the way to slot 6.

3->3(coll->5)</text><text x='5' y='125' class='sub ink'>23->3(coll->6), 5->5(coll->7), 15->5(coll->9)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hash table of size 10 (indices 0-9). Final state: index 0=empty, 1=empty, 2=12, 3=13, 4=2, 5=3, 6=23, 7=5, 8=18, 9=15. A cluster forms from index 2 to 9.

given.aim: Trace all 8 insertions and determine the final hash table layout

terms.connects: Earlier insertions block slots and force later keys to probe further

terms.meaning: Home slot is the last digit of k

given.plan: Insert each key in order: compute h(k) = k mod 10, place at that slot if free, otherwise probe linearly (slot+1, slot+2, ...) mod 10 until a free slot is found.

steps.title: Insert keys 12 and 18 (no collision)
steps.apply:
h(12) = 2, slot 2 is free -> table[2] = 12
h(18) = 8, slot 8 is free -> table[8] = 18
Table so far: [-, -, 12, -, -, -, -, -, 18, -]
steps.note: First two insertions go directly to their home slots.

steps.title: Insert key 13 and key 2
steps.apply:
h(13) = 3, slot 3 is free -> table[3] = 13
h(2) = 2, slot 2 is taken (12) -> try slot 3: taken (13) -> try slot 4: free -> table[4] = 2
Table: [-, -, 12, 13, 2, -, -, -, 18, -]
steps.note: Key 2 probes 2 extra steps due to collision.

steps.title: Insert key 3 and key 23
steps.apply:
h(3) = 3, slot 3 taken (13) -> slot 4 taken (2) -> slot 5 free -> table[5] = 3
h(23) = 3, slot 3 taken -> slot 4 taken -> slot 5 taken (3) -> slot 6 free -> table[6] = 23
Table: [-, -, 12, 13, 2, 3, 23, -, 18, -]
steps.note: Primary clustering is building up: slots 2-6 are all occupied.

steps.title: Insert key 5 and key 15
steps.apply:
h(5) = 5, slot 5 taken (3) -> slot 6 taken (23) -> slot 7 free -> table[7] = 5
h(15) = 5, slot 5 taken -> slot 6 taken -> slot 7 taken (5) -> slot 8 taken (18) -> slot 9 free -> table[9] = 15
Final table: [-, -, 12, 13, 2, 3, 23, 5, 18, 15]
steps.note: Key 15 must probe all the way to slot 9 because the cluster now spans slots 2-8.

questions.formula_note: Linear probing with division hash h(k) = k mod m. Primary clustering effect: once a contiguous run forms, future insertions that land anywhere in that run extend it, making long probe sequences.
